did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep neural network-based AI model named COMPASS (COntext-aware Multi-scale tool for Pathologists Assessing SlideS) to classify whole slide images for the diagnosis of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and to distinguish it from histological mimics in dermatopathology.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper describes original experimental work where an AI algorithm (COMPASS) was trained on 1061 prospectively and retrospectively collected glass slides to diagnose basal cell carcinoma. It reports specific results including sensitivity (98.0%) and specificity (97.0%) on a test set of 246 slides, indicating it is an original diagnostic accuracy study rather than a review.

title: Artificial intelligence for basal cell carcinoma: diagnosis and distinction from histological mimics
brief_summary: This study developed the COMPASS deep neural network model to diagnose basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and distinguish it from histological mimics in dermatopathology. The algorithm was trained on 1,061 prospectively and retrospectively collected whole slide images, including 616 BCC cases and 445 non-BCC cases with various mimics. Evaluation on a test set of 246 slides demonstrated high diagnostic accuracy, achieving a sensitivity of 98.0% and a specificity of 97.0%. The model correctly classified 240 slides, with only three false positives and three false negatives. These results indicate that AI can effectively account for morphological variation and assist pathologists in routine surgical practice.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for diagnosis; 616 BCC slides were collected and included in the training set, and 147 BCC slides were in the test set.

all_task_types.target: Presence of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) vs. histological mimics
all_task_types.details: The COMPASS deep neural network model was trained to classify whole slide images (WSIs) and patches as containing BCC or not. The task involved distinguishing BCC from various histological mimics (benign and malignant). The model provided a probability of BCC being present at the patch and slide levels, with performance evaluated via sensitivity (98.0%) and specificity (97.0%) on a test set of 246 slides.

datasets_used.name: Sullivan Nicolaides Pathology WSI Cohort
datasets_used.description: A collection of prospectively and retrospectively collected glass slides from Sullivan Nicolaides Pathology, digitally scanned to create whole slide images (WSIs). The dataset includes BCC cases and various histological mimics (benign and malignant) selected to reflect routine pathology practice and diagnostically challenging cases.
classes_used: Non-BCC (histological mimics including basaloid hyperplasia, native adnexal epithelium, desmoplastic trichoepithelioma, trichoblastoma, and Merkel cell carcinoma)

use.training: Used to train the COMPASS deep neural network model. The slides were divided into patches (87,205 labelled BCC present and 1,688,697 labelled BCC absent) for model training.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the model's diagnostic accuracy. The test set comprised 246 slides (147 containing BCC and 99 non-BCC).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Diagnostic accuracy was reported using sensitivity (98.0%) and specificity (97.0%) on a test set of 246 slides.

Sullivan Nicolaides Pathology WSI Cohort.notes: The model demonstrated high accuracy in distinguishing BCC from histological mimics. It correctly classified 240 out of 246 whole slide images, with only three false positives and three false negatives.

architecture_used.main_model: COMPASS (COntext-aware Multi-scale tool for Pathologists Assessing SlideS)
architecture_used.input_size: Patches representing a tissue area of 65,535 mm2

architecture_details.description: Deep neural network based on Convolutional Neural Networks

training_details.dataset_size: 87,205 patches labelled BCC present and 1,688,697 patches labelled BCC absent

classification_or_output_head.description: Provides a probability of BCC being present at the patch level and the slide level

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: COMPASS (COntext-aware Multi-scale tool for Pathologists Assessing SlideS)
preprocessing_techniques: Digital scanning of glass slides to create whole slide images (WSIs)
Division of WSIs into patches representing a tissue area of 65,535 mm2
conclusions: The COMPASS deep neural network model demonstrated high diagnostic accuracy in distinguishing basal cell carcinoma (BCC) from various histological mimics, achieving a sensitivity of 98.0% and a specificity of 97.0% on a test set of 246 whole slide images.
The AI model correctly classified 240 out of 246 whole slide images, with only three false positives and three false negatives.
The study serves as a proof of concept demonstrating that AI can account for morphological variation within BCC and accurately distinguish it from histologically similar entities.

The findings highlight the potential for artificial intelligence to play a valuable role in routine surgical pathology practice, particularly in diagnostically challenging cases where it may assist human pathologists alongside traditional ancillary tests.
important_remarks: The study is a proof-of-concept using a single-institution dataset (Sullivan Nicolaides Pathology) without external validation, limiting generalizability to other populations or scanning platforms.

The dataset includes prospectively collected BCC cases to reflect routine practice, but the non-BCC mimics were selected retrospectively, potentially introducing selection bias towards diagnostically challenging cases.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported; diagnostic accuracy is presented as point estimates (sensitivity 98.0%, specificity 97.0%) on a relatively small test set (n=246).

The model was trained on patches (87,205 BCC-present and 1,688,697 BCC-absent) aggregated to slide-level probabilities, but the paper does not detail how patch-level predictions were aggregated or if slide-level labels were consistent with patch-level ground truth.

The study acknowledges that BCC diagnosis can be difficult in slides with low tumor volume, architectural distortion (e.g., curettage specimens), or obscuring inflammation, which may represent specific failure modes not fully quantified.

The AI-human interface is noted as a critical area for future work, emphasizing that AI results should allow pathologists to reconcile their visual impression with algorithmic predictions rather than replace them.
The study focuses on distinguishing BCC from histological mimics rather than clinical mimics (e.g., seborrheic keratosis), limiting its direct applicability to pre-biopsy clinical decision support.
